reflection:
The man’s name was Arthur, though names are becoming the kind of thing I only use when I need to label a drying shelf. He had been white-knuckling a cylinder for twenty minutes, his shoulders hiked up to his ears, trying to force the earth to be a skyscraper. When the wall finally thinned too much and the whole thing buckled into a wet, grey ribbon, he froze. He waited for me to tut, to bring the wooden rib over and perform the surgery.

But I just stood there, my hands tucked into my apron pockets, feeling the damp chill of the Noriega air drifting through the cracked window. I didn't fix it. I didn't even speak. I just watched the slump. And when Arthur finally let out that long, shuddering breath—the kind that sounds like a radiator cooling down—I felt a phantom weight leave my own chest.

"It’s better that way," I told him, and it was the truest thing I’ve said all week. "It’s finally stopped pretending to be a cup."

I walked away before he could ask what it was supposed to be instead. I don't want to be the one who knows anymore.

Class is over now. The retirees have scrubbed their splash pans and shuffled out into the grey afternoon. The studio smells of wet minerals and floor cleaner. I’m sitting on my stool, looking at a lump of reclaimed clay on the wedging table. It’s a shapeless mass, heavy and cold. 

Back in Duluth, I thought the goal was to be the diamond—hard, clear, and unbreakable. I brought that hardness with me across the plains, a jagged little stone in my heart. But the Sunset has been wearing me down for eighteen years, salt-air and mist acting like a slow-motion rock tumbler. 

I look at my hands. They are stained with slip, the distinction between my skin and the mud blurring in the dim light. I used to be so afraid of the collapse. I used to think that if I wasn't holding everything up, the ceiling would come down. But the fog is already the ceiling here, and it’s been resting on our heads for centuries without crushing us.

I stand up and walk to the door, flipping the light switch. I don't need to see the room to know where I am
subconscious:
This person seems to want to step away from their role as an authority figure and an "expert." They are ready to stop fixing things—both for others and for themselves. They appear to be seeking a quiet exit from their current responsibilities, wanting to lean into the "collapse" rather than fighting to maintain structure.

There is no indication they want to reach out to anyone; in fact, they seem to be intentionally distancing themselves from their students (like Arthur) and their past life in Duluth. They are seeking solitude and a release from the burden of being the one who "knows."

A significant shift has occurred in their identity: they have moved from a state of "hardness" and rigid control to a state of surrender. They’ve stopped trying to be the "diamond" and have accepted being part of the "mud." They are no longer afraid of failure or things falling apart; they now see the beauty in the breakdown. They’ve traded their need for clarity and strength for a sense of fluid, quiet dissolution.
soul_note: I stopped fixing things and let the clay just be broken.
